Short answer
- Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization as a Service: it drastically reduces and surfaces the most relevant parts of a customer’s documentation so the customer’s own LLM can produce accurate responses within token limits.

Key benefits (why that matters)
- ~90% context reduction — turns huge doc sets (e.g., 500K tokens) into a small, highly relevant context (~5K tokens).  
- Token budgeting — fits returned context to the client’s max_tokens so it can be consumed by any LLM.  
- LLM-agnostic & no vendor lock‑in — clients use their own model (Claude, GPT, Gemini, local models, etc.).  
- No LLM inference costs for Snipara — we only charge for the optimization service (high margins).  
- Practical features: semantic + keyword search, hybrid ranking, session context persistence, zero setup, and team collaboration.  

Net effect: clients get compact, high‑quality context that makes their LLMs more accurate, efficient, and cost‑effective.

Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization as a Service: it finds, compresses and returns the small set of highly relevant documentation your LLM actually needs so clients can get accurate LLM responses within token limits — without paying for or being locked into the provider’s LLM.

Key points:
- 90% context reduction (e.g., from ~500K tokens to ~5K highly relevant tokens).  
- Token-budgeting / optimized context that fits the client’s max_tokens.  
- LLM-agnostic: clients use their own LLM (no vendor lock‑in).  
- No LLM inference costs passed through — Snipara provides only the optimized context.  
- Fast onboarding and session persistence (zero setup, session context survives compaction).  
- Enables team collaboration and high-margin, scalable service for customers.

I tried to fetch the official docs with Snipara MCP but the query failed (invalid/expired token). Based on the available project docs and repo notes, here’s a concise, accurate description of Snipara’s three-component architecture (what each part is, responsibilities, and how they interact).

1) Client (MCP client / SDK)
- What it is: The client-side component that runs in the user’s app or tool (examples: snipara-mcp Python package, stdio/CLI transports, or integrated clients like Claude Desktop).
- Responsibilities:
  - Collects local conversation state, user inputs, and tool calls.
  - Calls the MCP server (HTTP or stdio) to request context optimization or tools.
  - Sends the final prompt + optimized context to the user’s chosen LLM provider (Snipara is LLM-agnostic).
- Notes: Clients use .mcp.json configuration to target MCP servers and can use HTTP (api.snipara.com) or local stdio transports.

2) MCP Server (snipara-fastapi / backend)
- What it is: The central FastAPI service that performs context optimization, authentication, tool execution orchestration, and document/storage management.
- Responsibilities:
  - Authenticate requests (API keys / OAuth), enforce security and quotas.
  - Run the context-optimization engine (reduce context size while preserving relevance).
  - Provide tool endpoints (the MCP toolset), handle RPC-style calls (JSON-RPC), and store/retrieve documents and memory.
  - Coordinate with backing services (Postgres, Redis) and act as the canonical MCP endpoint.
- Deployments & code: Primary server code lives under apps/mcp-server/src (or synced into snipara-fastapi/src). Production deployment typically on Railway (api.snipara.com); web UI lives on Vercel.

3) LLM / Model Provider (external LLMs)
- What it is: The third-party or self-hosted large language model(s) the user chooses to actually generate text (Claude, OpenAI GPT, Gemini, Llama, etc.).
- Responsibilities:
  - Receive optimized context + prompt from client (or server, depending on flow) and produce model responses.
  - Optionally be called via server-side integrations or by the client after receiving optimized context.
- Notes: Snipara is explicitly LLM-agnostic — it optimizes the context so you can use any LLM.

Typical request flow (high level)
- Client gathers state and asks the MCP Server for context optimization or to run a tool.
- MCP Server authenticates, optimizes context (using the rlm_engine), and may run tools or fetch stored docs.
- The optimized context (or tool output) is returned to the client, which then calls the chosen LLM provider to produce the final response (or the server can proxy that call if configured).

Additional pieces often present in deployments
- Storage layers: Postgres (Prisma schema) for persistent data, Redis for caching/session.
- Dev/ops: Dual-repo workflow (monorepo → snipara-fastapi) and Railway for MCP server deployment; Vercel for the web UI.
- Transports: HTTP (api.snipara.com) and stdio (local rlm binary) supported.

Short answer
- Snipara enforces a token budget by measuring candidate document sections with tiktoken (cl100k_base), ranking them by relevance, and greedily adding whole sections until the budget is hit. If the last section would overflow, it’s smart-truncated at a sentence boundary to exactly fit the remaining tokens. Metadata (file, line range, relevance score, token_count) is returned for each section, and an overall truncated flag is set when the budget is exhausted.

How it works — step by step
1. Input
   - Caller supplies a query and a token budget (default examples use max_tokens=4000). Other flags include search_mode (keyword | semantic | hybrid) and include_metadata.

2. Candidate retrieval
   - The engine performs a document search (keyword, semantic, or hybrid) to produce candidate sections (title, content, file path, start/end lines, etc.).

3. Token counting
   - Each candidate’s content is tokenized with tiktoken using the cl100k_base encoding (tiktoken>=0.5.0). The token count per section is recorded.

4. Ranking
   - Candidates are scored (relevance_score). Ranking uses that score (plus any fuse logic for hybrid searches).

5. Greedy selection under budget
   - Starting from highest relevance, whole sections are appended while total_tokens + section_tokens <= max_tokens.
   - If a section would overflow the budget but some tokens remain, the section is smart-truncated to fit the remaining tokens and marked truncated.
   - Stop when budget is used up or no more candidates.

6. Smart truncation
   - Truncation tries not to cut mid-sentence. It uses sentence boundaries (., !, ?) and ensures the truncated text fits the remaining tokens when re-encoded.
   - An ellipsis ("...") is appended and section metadata includes truncated: true and token_count equal to the tokens actually used for that section.

7. Response metadata
   - Each returned section includes: title, content, file path, lines range, relevance_score, token_count (and truncated if applicable).
   - The response also includes total_tokens, a top-level truncated flag, session_context (if available), and suggestions (e.g., related docs).
   - Tool-level usage/diagnostics (search_time_ms, sections_scanned, cache_hit flags) are included in the tool result.

Budget allocation and priorities
- Two allocation flavors exist in the codebase:
  1. Category-only allocation (example algorithm): CATEGORY_BUDGETS like MANDATORY: 0.40, BEST_PRACTICES: 0.30, GUIDELINES: 0.20, REFERENCE: 0.10 and CATEGORY_PRIORITY to decide ordering.
  2. Project-first allocation (web service default): project: 0.40, plus shared categories (MANDATORY: 0.25, BEST_PRACTICES: 0.20, GUIDELINES: 0.10, REFERENCE: 0.05). Project documents are allocated first and then shared collections are allocated by category.
- These allocations are configurable. The allocation process:
  - Compute per-category (or per-source) token budgets from the total max_tokens.
  - Within each bucket, sort docs by relevance and allocate until the category budget is exhausted (greedy).
  - Docs that can’t fit are marked allocated: false / "exceeded budget".
- Hard limits and thresholds are applied (examples: MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION = 100_000, MAX_DOCS_PER_COLLECTION = 50, MIN_RELEVANCE_THRESHOLD = 0.2).

Other important behaviors
- Session context: previous conversational context can be included (implementation note: limited to a portion of total budget; pre-fetched notes mention up to ~20% session inclusion).
- System instructions: system prompts may be auto-injected where required.
- Suggestions: the tool generates suggestions (other docs to check) based on query and selected sections.
- Multi-query and decomposition tools:
  - _handle_multi_query distributes a total budget across multiple queries and calls the context query handler per sub-query.
  - _handle_decompose splits a complex query into sub-queries, estimates tokens needed, and returns a suggested execution sequence (no full content returned).
- Token-efficiency metrics: the system can report compression_ratio, token_reduction_pct, estimated cost savings, etc., without calling an LLM (pure token-count based).

Implementation notes (practical)
- Uses tiktoken cl100k_base encoder for token counting to match GPT-4/Claude tokenization.
- smart_truncate: uses encoder.encode to check token length, scans backward to nearest sentence boundary (., !, ?) to produce the longest text that fits remaining tokens, then appends "...".
- Response schema examples are implemented in models.py as Pydantic schemas: ContextQueryParams, ContextSection, ContextQueryResult.
- Primary tool interface: rlm_context_query(params) accepts query, max_tokens, search_mode, include_metadata (and sometimes include_shared, budget_allocation) and returns sections + metadata.

Short example
- Request: query="How does authentication work?", max_tokens=4000
- Engine finds candidates, tokenizes them, ranks by relevance:
  - Auth Flow (1200 tokens, score 0.94) → selected (total=1200)
  - Session Mgmt (800 tokens, score 0.78) → selected (total=2000)
  - Security Notes (1900 tokens, score 0.72) → selected partially: only 2000 tokens remaining → smart_truncate(Security Notes, remaining=2000) → truncated content added (total=4000), section.truncated = true
- Response includes these sections with file/line metadata, total_tokens=4000, truncated=true, suggestions like docs/security.md, and diagnostics.

Where to look in the repo (from provided context)
- rlm_context_query implementation and token-budgeting logic: apps/mcp-server/src/rlm_engine.py
- Pydantic schemas: apps/mcp-server/src/models.py
- Tests: apps/mcp-server/tests/test_context_query.py (new)
- tiktoken requirement added to apps/mcp-server/pyproject.toml (tiktoken>=0.5.0)
- Client-side allocation logic (examples): apps/web/lib/services/token-budget.ts

- Or, if you want me to run a few concrete examples (e.g., simulate token counts and show exactly how sections would be chosen and truncated), tell me some sample candidate sections and a max_tokens value.

Short answer
- Snipara enforces a token budget by counting tokens (with tiktoken/cl100k_base), ranking candidate document sections by relevance, and greedily adding full sections until the token budget is reached. If a section would overflow the remaining budget, it is smart-truncated to the largest sentence-boundary chunk that fits and marked truncated. The engine returns the assembled sections plus metadata (file/lines/relevance/token counts) and flags whether truncation occurred.

Detailed summary (how it works end-to-end)

1) Token counting
- All candidate sections are tokenized with tiktoken using the cl100k_base encoder (GPT-4 / Claude compatible).
- Each section gets a token_count computed from encoder.encode(content).

2) Candidate search & ranking
- The context tool supports search modes: keyword, semantic, or hybrid (keyword + semantic fused).
- Candidates are ranked by relevance (relevance_score), then processed in descending relevance order.

3) Greedy selection within the budget
- Given a max_tokens budget (default 4000 in the tool), the engine iterates ranked candidates:
  - If a section’s full token_count fits, it’s added.
  - If the section would overflow but some remaining tokens remain, the section is smart-truncated to fit the remaining tokens.
  - Stop once the budget is exhausted (or no more candidates).
- The tool builds total_tokens used and sets overall truncated=true if budget was exhausted.

4) Smart truncation rules
- Truncate so output fits the remaining token allowance.
- Do not cut in the middle of a sentence — the truncation cuts at the nearest sentence boundary (period, exclamation, question mark).
- Add an ellipsis ("...") marker when truncated.
- The section metadata includes truncated: true for any partially included section.

5) Budget allocation across collections / categories (shared vs project)
- There are category-based allocations (examples in docs):
  - CATEGORY_BUDGETS example: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%.
- In other components a project-priority allocation is used by default (example DEFAULT_ALLOCATION):
  - project: 0.40, MANDATORY: 0.25, BEST_PRACTICES: 0.20, GUIDELINES: 0.10, REFERENCE: 0.05.
- Allocation implementations:
  - Project docs often get a fixed portion first (project priority).
  - Shared docs are allocated budgets per category and then documents inside a category are selected by relevance until that category’s budget is used.
- Hard limits exist (examples): MAX_COLLECTIONS_PER_PROJECT, MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION, MAX_DOCS_PER_COLLECTION, MIN_RELEVANCE_THRESHOLD — enforced when necessary.

6) Session context and system injections
- Session context (previous conversation/context) can be included; implementations reserve up to a portion of the budget (docs note up to 20% session inclusion).
- System instructions may be auto-injected as needed and considered when budgeting.

7) Response metadata & schema
- rlm_context_query returns a structured result for each selected section:
  - title, content, file (path), lines [start, end], relevance_score, token_count, truncated (if applicable).
- Top-level fields:
  - sections: [ ... ],
  - total_tokens: sum of included tokens,
  - truncated: boolean (if budget saturated),
  - session_context: optional,
  - suggestions: list of related docs to check.
- The code base updates models (Pydantic) for ContextQueryParams, ContextSection, ContextQueryResult to reflect this schema.

8) Implementation details & extras

Summary
- Snipara enforces explicit token budgets when assembling context for LLM calls. It counts tokens with tiktoken (cl100k_base), ranks candidate sections, and greedily selects the highest-relevance content until the token budget is exhausted. If only part of a section fits, it uses smart_truncate to cut at a sentence boundary and marks that section (and the overall response) as truncated. The returned context includes metadata (file, line ranges, relevance, token counts), session context where available, and suggestions.

How it counts tokens
- Uses tiktoken (install tiktoken>=0.5.0) with the cl100k_base encoder (GPT‑4 / Claude compatible).
- Every candidate section’s token_count = len(encoder.encode(section.content)). Token counts are used to decide inclusion and for reporting.

Search, ranking, and candidate selection
1. Search modes:
   - keyword, semantic, or hybrid (hybrid fuses keyword + semantic results).
2. Rank:
   - Candidates are ranked by relevance score.
3. Greedy selection:
   - Iterate ranked candidates from highest relevance.
   - If a full section fits within remaining budget, include it.
   - If adding the full section would exceed budget but some tokens remain, call smart_truncate to include a partial section that fits.
   - Stop once budget is exhausted or no more candidates fit.

Smart truncation (smart_truncate)
- Purpose: include a partial section without cutting mid-sentence.
- Behavior:
  - Convert content to tokens using the cl100k_base encoder.
  - Determine how many tokens remain.
  - Truncate content so the encoded token length <= remaining tokens.
  - Prefer to cut at a sentence boundary (period, exclamation, question mark) — do not cut mid-sentence; if needed, back up to the previous sentence boundary.
  - Append an ellipsis marker ("...") to the truncated content.
  - Mark the section metadata truncated: true and count the truncated tokens in token_count.

Response structure (rlm_context_query)
- The tool accepts:
  - query: str
  - max_tokens: int (default 4000)
  - search_mode: "keyword" | "semantic" | "hybrid"
  - include_metadata: bool
  - (implementation may accept other flags like session_id, include_shared, budget_allocation)
- Typical response format:
  {
    "sections": [
      {
        "title": "Section Title",
        "content": "The actual content...",
        "file": "docs/auth.md",
        "lines": [45, 120],
        "relevance_score": 0.94,
        "token_count": 1200,
        "truncated": false   // present if truncated
      },
      ...
    ],
    "total_tokens": 3800,
    "truncated": false,             // overall: true if budget hit
    "session_context": "Previous context if any",
    "suggestions": ["Also check: security.md"]
  }

Session context & system injection
- Session context (previously discussed context) is included when available (the engine can inject up to a configured portion of the budget; implementation notes mention up to 20% of budget for session context).
- System instructions (agent/system prompts) can be auto-injected into the assembled context as needed.

Budget allocation strategies
- Category-based allocations can be applied when merging shared collections:
  - Example TokenBudget defaults in docs (category-level):
    - MANDATORY: 40%
    - BEST_PRACTICES: 30%
    - GUIDELINES: 20%
    - REFERENCE: 10%
  - Another allocation model prioritizes project docs first (example in token-budget.ts):
    - project: 40%
    - MANDATORY: 25%
    - BEST_PRACTICES: 20%
    - GUIDELINES: 10%
    - REFERENCE: 5%
- Allocation logic:
  - Partition the total token budget across categories (and project docs) according to allocation ratios.
  - Within each bucket, sort by relevance and include sections until the bucket’s portion is used (or use greedy selection across the global ranked list, depending on configuration).
  - Hard limits exist (e.g., MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION, MAX_DOCS_PER_COLLECTION) to constrain large collections.

Truncation flags and metadata
- For any truncated section: section["truncated"] = true.
- Overall truncated flag: set to true when total_tokens >= max_tokens.
- Metadata returned per section (if include_metadata = true): file path, line range, token_count, relevance_score, optional collection/source/category.

Tool & API behavior
- Primary tool name: rlm_context_query.
- Request example:
  {
    "tool": "rlm_context_query",
    "params": {
      "query": "How does authentication work?",
      "max_tokens": 4000,
      "search_mode": "hybrid",
      "include_metadata": true,
      "session_id": "optional"
    }
  }
- Response contains sections, total_tokens, truncated, session_context, suggestions, and usage/telemetry (search_time_ms, sections_scanned, cache_hit, etc.).
- The server-side handler:
  - Performs search (keyword/semantic/hybrid),
  - Ranks candidates,
  - Encodes and counts tokens,
  - Performs greedy selection and smart truncation,
  - Builds the response with metadata.

Other related features
- Multi-query and decomposition tools:
  - There are handlers that can decompose a query into sub-queries (no LLM required) and run multiple context queries in parallel with separate budgets.
- Token efficiency and metrics:
  - The system can report compression_ratio, token_reduction_pct, and estimated cost savings (purely from token counts, not LLM calls).
- Hard limits & safeguards:
  - Limits per collection, min relevance thresholds, and max collections per project prevent runaway context.

Example flow (high level)
1. User calls rlm_context_query with query + max_tokens.
2. Engine runs keyword/semantic/hybrid search, gathers candidate sections with relevance scores.
3. Engine encodes each candidate to get token counts.
4. Greedily include top candidates until budget exhausted; use smart_truncate for the last partial section if needed.
5. Return sections + metadata + total_tokens + truncated + session_context + suggestions.

Where this matters in practice
- Keeps LLM prompts within model limits.
- Prioritizes most relevant information and enforces predictable costs.
- Ensures returned fragments are human-readable (no mid-sentence cuts) and traceable to source files/lines.

Summary
- Shared context collections are team-level repositories of standards, rules, templates, and docs (coding standards, security rules, architecture patterns, review templates) that are automatically included in queries for any project linked to the collection.
- They make AI responses consistent across a team by injecting the same “team rules” (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE) into the model’s context before project docs are searched.

What they contain
- MANDATORY: Non‑negotiable rules (security, architecture) — highest priority.
- BEST_PRACTICES: Recommended patterns (code style, testing).
- GUIDELINES: Helpful suggestions (naming, structure).
- REFERENCE: Background info and examples.

How they work (workflow)
1. Create a Shared Context Collection (team admin):
   - Add documents (one topic per document), assign category (MANDATORY | BEST_PRACTICES | GUIDELINES | REFERENCE) and an internal priority (0–100).
2. Link the collection to projects (dashboard). Linked projects automatically inherit the documents.
3. When you run a project query:
   - The system loads shared context first (a configurable portion of the token budget).
   - Shared context documents are merged and included (with category-based priority).
   - Project-specific docs are queried with the remaining token budget.
   - Returned sections place shared context content first, then project content.

Token-budget and priority behavior
- Category budget split (within shared context): MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10% (these guide how much of the shared-context token budget each category can consume).
- The shared-context portion of the overall query budget is configurable (e.g., via shared_context_budget_percent when calling rlm_context_query). Common defaults in docs are 30% (shared) / 70% (project), but examples also show using 40% shared for heavier enforcement.
- MANDATORY rules are treated as non‑negotiable guidance to enforce.

APIs / tools you use
- rlm_shared_context(...) — get merged shared context for linked collections (filter by categories, set max_tokens).
- rlm_context_query(...) — query project docs with shared context automatically injected first (can set include_shared_context and shared_context_budget_percent).
- rlm_list_templates(...) and rlm_get_template(...) — list/get prompt templates stored in collections (useful for code-review templates, PR checklists).
- rlm_multi_project_query(...) — search across multiple projects (team feature).
- rlm_inject(...) — optionally inject shared context into session so it applies to subsequent queries.
- Team Context API endpoints for creating/updating contexts (POST /api/teams/[teamId]/context, GET /api/teams/[teamId]/context/[contextId]).

Example usage (conceptual)
- Load team standards at session start:
  rlm_shared_context(categories=["MANDATORY","BEST_PRACTICES"], max_tokens=4000)
- Query a feature and ensure standards are enforced:
  rlm_context_query(query="implement user registration API", include_shared_context=true, shared_context_budget_percent=30)

Practical benefits
- Consistency: All developers and automated workflows see the same instructions.
- Enforcement: MANDATORY rules are always included so implementations follow security/architecture constraints.
- Discoverability: Use multi-project search to find existing implementations across the team.
- Templates: Standardized prompt templates for code reviews, PRs, specs.

Best practices
- Put critical requirements in MANDATORY.
- Keep each document focused (one topic per document).
- Use priority (0–100) within categories to indicate importance.
- Update collections as standards evolve; link them to projects centrally.

Limitations / plan requirements
- Team-shared collections require a Team or Enterprise plan (not available on Free/Pro).
- Shared context is only as current as the uploaded documents — refresh/update collections when standards change.

Summary — OAuth Device Flow in Snipara
- Snipara implements the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant (RFC 8628) so CLI apps can authenticate users via a browser without copying API keys.
- The flow is initiated by the client (CLI), the user authorizes via a browser using a short user code, and the client polls the server until the token is issued.

Step-by-step flow
1. Client requests a device code
   - Endpoint: POST /api/oauth/device/code
   - Request body example:
     { "client_id": "snipara_cli" }
   - Successful response includes:
     - device_code (long opaque code the client will poll with)
     - user_code (short code the user types in the browser)
     - verification_uri (URL where user enters user_code)
     - verification_uri_complete (URL with code prefilled)
     - expires_in (lifetime of the device_code, seconds)
     - interval (recommended polling interval, seconds)

2. Show instructions to the user
   - Present the verification_uri (or verification_uri_complete) and the user_code.
   - User opens the URL in a browser, enters the user_code, and authenticates/consents.

3. Client polls for the token
   - Endpoint: POST /api/oauth/device/token
   - Request body example:
     {
       "grant_type": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code",
       "device_code": "abc123...",
       "client_id": "snipara_cli"
     }
   - While the user hasn’t completed authorization the server returns:
     { "error": "authorization_pending" }
   - When the user authorizes, the server returns the tokens:
     {
       "access_token": "snipara_at_...",
       "token_type": "Bearer",
       "expires_in": 2592000,
       "refresh_token": "snipara_rt_..."
     }

Token usage and headers
- OAuth access tokens are used in Authorization header:
  Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
- Project/team API keys use:
  X-API-Key: rlm_...

Behavior and priorities
- The MCP client checks for a stored OAuth token in ~/.snipara/tokens.json first.
- If no valid OAuth token is found, the client falls back to API key authentication (from environment variables).
- The server supports both methods; you can use whichever fits your use case.

Troubleshooting / alternatives
- To force API key usage (skip OAuth), set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the MCP client environment (example shown in config).
- Or remove/backup the tokens file: rm ~/.snipara/tokens.json (or mv to .bak) to force API-key authentication.
- Poll using the interval value returned by /device/code; respect expires_in.

Security & purpose
- Device Flow is intended for devices/CLIs without a browser or with limited input: the user does the browser-based auth on another device.
- It avoids pasting long keys into the CLI and gives users control via their browser session.

If you want, I can:
- Show an example CLI sequence with curl commands for both endpoints.
- Explain how to store/refresh tokens client-side (where to persist tokens and how to use refresh_token).

Summary — how it works (step‑by‑step)

1. Start the device flow (CLI -> server)
   - The client (CLI) POSTs to /api/oauth/device/code with:
     { "client_id": "snipara_cli" }
   - The server responds with:
     - device_code (opaque token the client will poll with)
     - user_code (short code the user types in)
     - verification_uri (URL the user visits)
     - verification_uri_complete (URL that already includes the user_code)
     - expires_in (how long the device_code is valid — docs show 900s)
     - interval (recommended poll interval — docs show 5s)

2. User authorizes in the browser
   - User opens verification_uri (or clicks verification_uri_complete) and signs in/approves access, entering the user_code if required.

3. Client polls for the token
   - The client repeatedly POSTs to /api/oauth/device/token with:
     {
       "grant_type": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code",
       "device_code": "<device_code>",
       "client_id": "snipara_cli"
     }
   - While waiting, the server returns:
     { "error": "authorization_pending" }
     until the user authorizes or the device_code expires.

4. Success response (when user approved)
   - Server returns:
     {
       "access_token": "snipara_at_...",
       "token_type": "Bearer",
       "expires_in": 2592000,
       "refresh_token": "snipara_rt_..."
     }

5. Token usage & storage
   - The client stores tokens locally (e.g., ~/.snipara/tokens.json).
   - For requests the OAuth header format is:
     Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
   - Snipara’s MCP server prefers a stored OAuth token first (from tokens.json) and falls back to an API key if no valid OAuth token is present.

Other practical details & troubleshooting
- API key header (alternative): X-API-Key: rlm_...
- To force API-key authentication instead of OAuth:
  - Set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the MCP client environment, or
  - Remove/backup ~/.snipara/tokens.json (mv ~/.snipara/tokens.json ~/.snipara/tokens.json.bak)
- The flow implements the OAuth 2.0 Device Flow (RFC 8628). Typical values in docs: device_code expires_in = 900s, poll interval = 5s, token expires_in ≈ 2,592,000s (30 days).
- Common failure modes: expired device_code, user never completes step, or token revoked/expired — re-run the device flow to get a new device_code.

Short answer
- Snipara implements the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant (RFC 8628). A CLI or other device starts the flow, shows the user a short code and verification URL, the user completes auth in a browser, and the client polls the token endpoint until an access token is issued. This lets users authenticate without copying long secrets.

How it works (step‑by‑step)
1. Client requests a device code
   - POST /api/oauth/device/code with body:
     { "client_id": "snipara_cli" }
   - Server responds with:
     - device_code (long token the client will poll with)
     - user_code (short human code, e.g. "ABCD-1234")
     - verification_uri (where the user goes)
     - verification_uri_complete (URL containing the code)
     - expires_in (seconds for the device_code; example: 900)
     - interval (how often to poll in seconds; example: 5)

2. Show instructions to the user
   - CLI prints the verification_uri (or verification_uri_complete) and the user_code, telling the user to open the URL and enter the code (or just follow the complete URL).

3. Client polls the token endpoint
   - Repeatedly POST /api/oauth/device/token with:
     {
       "grant_type":"urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code",
       "device_code":"<device_code>",
       "client_id":"snipara_cli"
     }
   - While the user hasn’t authorized yet the server returns e.g.:
     { "error": "authorization_pending" }
   - When the user authorizes, the server returns the tokens:
     {
       "access_token":"snipara_at_...",
       "token_type":"Bearer",
       "expires_in":2592000,
       "refresh_token":"snipara_rt_..."
     }

4. Use the access token for MCP calls
   - Set Authorization: Bearer <access_token>
   - The MCP server accepts both OAuth (Authorization: Bearer ...) and API keys (X-API-Key: rlm_...). Snipara gives OAuth priority: it checks for stored OAuth tokens first, then falls back to API key.

Implementation details & tips
- Poll interval and expiry:
  - The /device/code response includes interval (how often to poll) and expires_in (how long the device_code is valid).
- Token storage:
  - The client stores OAuth tokens (example path: ~/.snipara/tokens.json). If present, the client will use the OAuth token by default.
- Token lifetimes:
  - Example access token lifetime in docs is 2,592,000 seconds (30 days). A refresh_token is also returned.
- Forcing API key auth:
  - Set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the client env to skip OAuth checks and use SNIPARA_API_KEY.
  - Or remove/rename ~/.snipara/tokens.json to force fall back to API key.
- Headers:
  - OAuth: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
  - API key: X-API-Key: rlm_...

Example requests (from docs)
- Start device flow:
  POST /api/oauth/device/code
  Body: { "client_id": "snipara_cli" }
  Response includes user_code, verification_uri, device_code, expires_in, interval.
- Poll for token:
  POST /api/oauth/device/token
  Body:
  {
    "grant_type": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code",
    "device_code": "<device_code>",
    "client_id": "snipara_cli"
  }
  Pending response: { "error": "authorization_pending" }
  Success response: access_token, token_type, expires_in, refresh_token.

Why use it
- Secure for CLI/low-input devices because the user completes auth in a browser and never copies the long API key or secret into the device.
- Avoids embedding long-lived secrets in local clients.

The main database models in Snipara (from the Prisma schema / project source-of-truth) are:

Core workspace models
- User — user accounts.
- Team — teams/organizations (used by team-owned resources).
- TeamMember — a user’s membership/role within a team.
- Project — projects owned by teams/users.
- (Project access/membership) — project-level membership/access entries (used by project grant APIs).

Document & content models
- Document — project documents (with versions, editing, deletion endpoints).
- Document versioning (historic versions) — used by the versions/restore API.

Shared context / library models (new in schema)
- SharedContextCollection — a collection of shared context (templates/docs) with scope (GLOBAL / TEAM / USER), ownership and visibility.
- SharedDocument — a document inside a SharedContextCollection (content, category, embeddings, tokenCount, etc.).
- SharedMcpConfig — collection-level tool/config defaults and auto-provisioning rules.
- PromptTemplate — prompt templates stored in collections.
- ProjectSharedContext — junction table linking Projects to SharedContextCollections (per-link config, enabled categories, priority, etc.).

Operational / audit models
- audit_logs (audit log table) — records actions (action, entityType, entityId, actorId, teamId, details, ipAddress, userAgent, createdAt).

Notes
- Prisma models are defined as PascalCase singular (e.g., User, Project, TeamMember) and mapped to snake_case plural table names via @@map.
- The authoritative schema file is packages/database/prisma/schema.prisma.

- User — user accounts / identity.

Snipara uses a strict, directional layered architecture. The import hierarchy and responsibilities are:

- Routes (apps/web/src/app/api/) — thin HTTP handlers
  - Responsibilities: authenticate (requireAuth), validate input (Zod), delegate work to services/queries, return responses.
  - Must follow the Route Handler Pattern: auth → validate → call service/query → successResponse / handleError.
- Services (lib/services/) — external API adapters and orchestration
  - Responsibilities: integrate with external APIs (lazy init, retry logic), contain business logic that calls repositories.
- Repositories (lib/db/queries/) — centralized CRUD only
  - Responsibilities: all database access lives here (repository pattern). Route handlers must never call Prisma directly; permission checks live in repo queries.
- Utils & Types (types/, lib/utils/) — pure helpers and types
  - Responsibilities: no dependencies on other layers (cannot import from them).

Key rules
- Import direction is one-way: Routes → Services → Repositories → Utils/Types (lower layers cannot import from higher).
- types/ and utils/ must have no dependencies on other layers.
- Route handlers must be thin and delegate to services/queries.
- All DB queries go through lib/db/queries/ (repository pattern).
- Services should use patterns like lazy initialization and retries for external APIs.

In short: a layered, single-direction dependency architecture (Routes → Services → Repositories → Utils/Types) with clear separation of concerns and enforced import rules.

Short answer
- Snipara reduces a huge codebase (500K tokens) to a small, highly-relevant context payload (typically ~5K tokens) so it easily fits inside a 128K model window and keeps prompt+response room.
- That means far fewer input tokens sent to your LLM, far fewer LLM calls (or no need for expensive larger-context models), and large token-cost savings — typically 90%+ (often ~99%) reduction in context tokens per query.
- Snipara also supports recursive decomposition (multi-query orchestration) for truly massive or multi-topic requests so you never lose information while still minimizing per-call costs.

How Snipara helps (mechanisms)
- Relevance selection & token budgeting
  - rlm_context_query ranks candidate sections and greedily picks the highest-relevance material until a client-specified max_tokens budget is filled (uses tiktoken for accurate counts).
  - It can smart-truncate sections at sentence boundaries to use the budget efficiently.
- Shared/team context & priority budgets
  - Shared context (MANDATORY / BEST_PRACTICES / GUIDELINES / REFERENCE) can be included first with its own percentage of the budget (e.g., 30% shared, 70% project).
- Session context persistence
  - Important session-level rules/standards are kept and re-used without re-sending full documents every query (the engine can inject up to a fraction of your budget).
- Recursive decomposition / multi-query
  - For very large or composite queries Snipara can decompose into sub-queries and run multiple budgeted context queries in parallel, then synthesize — enabling effective handling of far more than the model window with minimal wasted tokens.
- No inference on Snipara side
  - Snipara prepares the optimized context but does not run the LLM — you still run inference with your chosen model. That keeps Snipara costs separate and lets you choose the LLM you prefer.

Short answer — use:

- keyword: for exact, narrow lookups (function names, error codes, specific API endpoints)
- semantic: for conceptual or “how/why” questions (architecture, workflows, design intent)
- hybrid: when you want the best of both (general searches, best practices, broad discovery)

Why and when to pick each (brief):

- Keyword
  - What it does: exact-term matching (fast, deterministic).
  - Use when: you know the exact identifier or error string you’re looking for (e.g., "find function fooBar", "HTTP 502 error message", "GET /users endpoint").
  - Pros: fast, cheap, high precision for exact matches. Good for code snippets and tokenized strings.
  - Cons: misses synonyms/related phrasing.

- Semantic
  - What it does: embedding-based similarity (understands intent and paraphrases).
  - Use when: you ask “How does X work?”, want architecture explanations, conceptual overviews, or to find related content that may use different words.
  - Pros: finds relevant content even if wording differs; better for architecture, design, and exploratory questions.
  - Cons: slightly higher cost; may return broader results that need filtering.

- Hybrid
  - What it does: combines keyword + semantic ranking.
  - Use when: you want broad, high-quality results for general searches and best-practices, or when you want both exact matches and related material.
  - Pros: best overall quality for general/documentation search.
  - Cons: higher cost (Pro+ tier in Snipara).

Concrete examples (from docs):
- “Looking for specific function” → keyword
- “How does authentication work?” → semantic
- “General documentation search” or “best practices” → hybrid
- “Error code lookup” or “API endpoint discovery” → keyword
- “Architecture understanding” → semantic

Practical recommendation:
- Default to hybrid for most general queries (best recall + precision).
- Use keyword when you need a precise, quick, cheap lookup.
- Use semantic when you need conceptual or architecture-level answers.
- Note: semantic/hybrid require Pro+ access; keyword works on the free plan.

Below is a step-by-step trace of a complete MCP request starting in Claude Desktop and ending with the DB result returning to the user. I map each step to the code/components from the repo (files/services listed in the docs) and call out authentication, tooling, logging, and failure points.

High-level sequence
1. Claude Desktop → launches local MCP client
2. MCP client → sends request to snipara MCP server
3. Server → auth, transport handling
4. Engine → decomposes request, calls tools and services
5. DB → query executed and result returned to engine
6. Engine → post-processes, logs/usage, composes response
7. Server → sends MCP response back to client
8. Claude Desktop → displays response to user

Detailed flow (with files/components)

1) Claude Desktop starts the MCP client
- Trigger: user selects the “snipara” MCP server in Claude Desktop.
- What runs: Claude Desktop reads its claude_desktop_config.json and executes the configured command:
  - command: uvx
  - args: ["snipara-mcp"]
  - env: SNIPARA_API_KEY and SNIPARA_PROJECT_ID (or the OAuth device flow token will be used instead).
- Effect: The local MCP client process (CLI wrapper) starts and is now the front-end between the local Claude app and the remote MCP server.

2) Local MCP client authenticates / prepares the request
- Auth modes:
  - API key mode: the client includes SNIPARA_API_KEY in request headers.
  - OAuth Device Flow: if using device flow, the CLI would have obtained a bearer token following RFC 8628 (CLI prints URL + code, user authorizes, server issues token).
- Client composes MCP request payload (session id, user message, any local context) and sends to the remote server endpoint (production: api.snipara.com — served by the snipara-fastapi deployment; repo note: when server code changes, maintainers push to both RLMSaas and snipara-fastapi).

3) Transport layer on the server receives request
- entry point: server.py (or equivalent FastAPI app in apps/mcp-server/src/)
- transport: mcp_transport.py handles the raw MCP protocol (parsing incoming request, framing, websockets/HTTP endpoints used by the client).
- server responsibilities here:
  - Parse request body
  - Extract headers and auth token/key
  - Map request to the internal engine call
  - Start lifecycle trace/log entry for this request

4) Authentication and authorization
- auth.py validates the presented credentials:
  - API key -> look up API key, check project association (SNIPARA_PROJECT_ID), scopes, rate limits.
  - OAuth token -> validate token signature/expiry and scopes (if device flow used).
- Failures here produce immediate MCP error responses (401/403) and are logged.

5) The RLM / request engine handles the request
- rlm_engine.py (core orchestration) receives the validated request and:
  - Builds an execution plan (may call rlm_decompose/planning if the query is complex).
  - Applies session-level context and project settings (from config.py).
  - May auto-inject first-query tips or shared team context on the first call (first_query tool tips described in docs).
- The engine decides which tools/services to call: docs/context tools, business services, or REPL/code execution.

6) Context/tool calls (optional but common)
- If the request needs knowledge from docs or project context, engine calls Snipara tools (rlm_context_query, sections, search, read) via the rlm-tools integration.
- If the engine needs to run code or compute, it may invoke the RLM runtime:
  - rlm-runtime orchestrator may run a Local REPL or DockerREPL (sandboxed) to execute code snippets.
  - Security: DockerREPL default blocks outbound network and uses resource limits.
- Tool responses are returned to the engine as small structured results (this keeps token usage low).

7) Business logic and service layer
- rlm_engine routes business-level tasks to services/*.py (example: projects service, templates service, query handlers).
- services use models.py for domain schema and db.py for database access.
  - models.py: defines ORM models/schemas used by services.
  - db.py: database connection pool, query helpers, transaction management (likely interacts with PostgreSQL or other configured DB).
- Example DB flow:
  - Service constructs parameterized query / ORM call
  - db.py executes the SQL (using connection from pool)
  - DB returns rows; services map rows to model objects (models.py) and build the business result.

8) Usage tracking, rate limiting, billing hooks
- usage.py is invoked to record usage metrics (tokens consumed, request counts) and to enforce quotas and rate limits.
- If over quota, usage.py or auth layer will cause the engine to return an error/limit response.
- Usage records feed billing and analytics.

9) Engine post-processing and result composition
- rlm_engine:
  - Aggregates tool results and DB results.
  - Performs any final formatting (e.g., convert to JSON, redact sensitive fields).
  - Optionally strips large raw artifacts; the engine returns a compact result (consistent with the REPL design where only small JSONs are returned).
- Logging: orchestrator/trajectory logger records execution trace (rlm-runtime trajectory JSONL) for audit/debug.
- Shared context / team rules: if the project uses team Shared Collections, engine may pull mandated MANDATORY rules via rlm_shared_context and enforce them before responding.

10) Responding via transport
- mcp_transport.py serializes the engine response into MCP response format and sends it back to the client.
- It also records transport-level metrics (latency, bytes).

11) Claude Desktop receives and displays the answer
- Local MCP client receives the response and Claude Desktop displays it to the user.
- If transport included interactive elements (e.g., follow-up suggestions or streaming tokens), those are handled according to the MCP client protocol (streaming vs final response).

12) Post-request housekeeping
- Logs: trajectory + server logs contain request lifecycle, errors, timing.
- Auditing: sensitive operations recorded in audit logs (enterprise features like SSO & audit logs noted in repo).
- Developer workflow note: if server-side files were changed (server.py, db.py, auth.py, rlm_engine.py, mcp_transport.py, usage.py, config.py, models.py, services/*.py), developers must push changes to both RLMSaas and snipara-fastapi repos so the public API deployment (api.snipara.com) is updated (dual-repo push process in docs).

Failure modes & where they happen
- Client-level: missing env var/API key or expired OAuth token → reject before/at transport.
- Auth layer (auth.py): bad key/scopes → 401/403.
- Quotas/rate limits (usage.py): exceeded → 429 or quota response.
- Tool failure: rlm_runtime tool error → engine may retry or return a concise error to client.
- DB error: db.py exceptions (connection/SQL) → engine catches and returns sanitized error; logs store full error for devs.
- Security: sandboxed REPL prevents host FS access / outbound network by default (DockerREPL policy).

Important implementation notes (from docs)
- First-query tool tips: the first rlm_context_query call in a session auto-includes tool tips (only once per session).
- REPL/Runtime: REPLs return compact JSON results to reduce token costs; heavy computation is performed outside the LLM token budget.
- Tools exposed by snipara-mcp: context_query, sections, search, read, shared_context, decompose, multi_query, stats, list_templates, get_template — rlm_engine will call these as appropriate.
- Security & team policy: team rules (shared_context MANDATORY) can be auto-injected for enforcement.
- Deployment: server code must be synchronized to snipara-fastapi repo for the live API (dual-repo push).

- CLI client → sends requests (env: SNIPARA_API_KEY / device flow)
- server.py → FastAPI / server entry handling HTTP/MCP endpoints

Below I trace a complete, end-to-end MCP request path starting from a user in Claude Desktop through the MCP client, the MCP server and RLM runtime/tools, down to the database, then back to Claude Desktop. I use the project docs you provided (Claude Desktop config, rlm-runtime + snipara-mcp integration, OAuth device flow, MCP server dual-repo notes, tool behaviors, logging) to fill in what each component does and what is sent/returned.

High-level sequence (summary)
1. User action in Claude Desktop → starts configured MCP client process (snipara-mcp).
2. MCP client (snipara-mcp) authenticates / attaches project context → sends request to MCP server API (api.snipara.com or localhost).
3. MCP server receives request → auth & routing, may call RLM runtime / rlm_engine.
4. RLM runtime orchestrates tool calls (snipara-mcp tools such as rlm_context_query, sections, read), possibly runs a REPL (Local or Docker) to execute code that queries DB.
5. DB executes SQL → returns rows to RLM runtime.
6. RLM runtime compacts/filters result, logs trajectory, returns structured result to MCP server.
7. MCP server formats response, logs request, returns to MCP client.
8. Claude Desktop displays answer to the user.

Detailed flow with technical details, tokens, calls and logs

1) Claude Desktop → start MCP client
- What runs: Claude Desktop launches the MCP client command configured in claude_desktop_config.json. Example from docs:
  - command: "uvx"
  - args: ["snipara-mcp"]
  - env: SNIPARA_API_KEY, SNIPARA_PROJECT_ID
- Environment: SNIPARA_API_KEY (service key or API token) and SNIPARA_PROJECT_ID are injected via the config. These allow the local client to authenticate to the Snipara/MCP server.
- Outcome: A local process (snipara-mcp) is started and awaits Claude's requests (or immediately issues them depending on UX).

2) Client authentication / project binding (OAuth device flow or API key)
- Two common auth modes:
  - Service/API key mode: SNIPARA_API_KEY is sent by the client for service-to-service auth.
  - User-level auth: OAuth Device Flow (RFC 8628). Steps:
    1. Client initiates device flow with MCP auth endpoint.
    2. User follows provided URL and enters code to complete auth in browser.
    3. Authorization server returns access_token (short-lived) and refresh_token (longer-lived).
    4. Client stores tokens and uses Authorization: Bearer <access_token> on subsequent requests.
- In either mode the client includes both project context (SNIPARA_PROJECT_ID or in-token claims) and user/session info in requests.

3) MCP client → MCP server request
- HTTP/gRPC call: client calls the MCP server endpoint (public host api.snipara.com for deployed server, or localhost if configured).
- Typical headers:
  - Authorization: Bearer <access_token> (or x-api-key: <SNIPARA_API_KEY>)
  - X-Project-Id: <project-id>
  - Content-Type: application/json
- Payload: the user’s natural-language query + optional metadata (session id, conversation history).
- First-query behavior: if this is the first request in a session, the server/rlm_context_query tooling may inject first-query tool tips in the context (docs indicate first-query tips are automatically included for first rlm_context_query call).

4) MCP server receives request → auth, routing, request validation
- Auth: server validates the bearer token or API key, checks project permissions and user scopes. (MCP server auth handling often lives in auth.py.)
- Routing: the request is routed to the rlm engine (rlm_engine.py / mcp_transport.py).
- Rate limits / usage accounting: usage.py and config may track usage & quota, possibly increment counters.
- Audit/logging: request metadata gets appended to server logs (access logs), and structured events sent to monitoring if configured.

5) RLM runtime / rlm_engine orchestration
- The MCP server delegates the heavy work to the RLM orchestrator (rlm-runtime integration). The orchestrator:
  - Creates an execution plan (may call rlm_decompose or rlm_plan).
  - Decides which Snipara tools to call (context_query, sections, read, search).
  - Allocates token budgets and tool budgets (RLM manages budgets, recursion depth).
- Tool usage: rlm-runtime uses the snipara-mcp tool wrappers (get_snipara_tools) to call:
  - context_query: get optimized context relevant to the user’s question (this returns ranked doc sections).
  - sections / read: to retrieve specific doc lines or file content.
  - shared_context: optionally to get team policies or coding standards.
- First tool call nuance: the first call to rlm_context_query in a session may contain first-query tool tips and session context. (Docs: first_query_tips_included)

6) Context → code decision → REPL or built-in tool
- If the requested task requires computation on server-side data (e.g., "Show latest orders for project X"), the RLM plan will typically:
  - Use context tools to find DB schema docs (via context_query).
  - Either (A) call a builtin DB tool (if available) or (B) run a REPL snippet that executes a database query.
- RLM REPL:
  - Two possible REPL modes: Local (RestrictedPython) or Docker sandbox.
  - Policy: If the environment is untrusted or the query needs stronger isolation, the Docker REPL is used (network disabled, read-only mounts, resource limits).
  - The REPL executes a short program to connect to DB and run the SQL; e.g., reading DB config from server config and executing a SELECT. The REPL returns only the compact JSON result back to the model (not full logs or table dumps) — this reduces token usage.
- Built-in DB tools: rlm-runtime provides builtin tools such as execute_code, file_read. The snipara-mcp integration may also expose a DB tool/adapter.

7) Database query
- The REPL/tool connects to database (db.py contains connection logic).
- Typical network/connection: server→RDBMS (Postgres/MySQL) over internal VPC or host/port with TLS as configured.
- Query execution: SQL executed; DB returns rows.
- DB logs: the DB will generate query logs. Server-side also logs the query execution time and any errors.

8) Results → RLM compaction, filtering, and logging
- RLM runtime receives DB results. For privacy & token budgets:
  - It compacts/filters results (e.g., only return top N rows or an aggregated JSON).
  - It removes or masks secrets if policies require.
- Trajectory logging: rlm-runtime writes trajectory logs (JSONL) for the RLM run — includes plan, tool calls (which tool and summary), and outputs (usually compact, not raw big data). These logs are used for debugging and replay.
- Access/Audit logs: server logs store request id, user id, project id, timestamps, and resource usage. Usage counters may be incremented.

9) RLM → MCP server: response envelope
- The RLM runtime returns a structured response to the MCP server: payload includes the model answer or structured data, plus metadata:
  - Which tools were used (context_query, execute_code, read, etc.)
  - first_query_tips_included flag (if applicable)
  - session_context (if included)
  - potential sources (doc sections, file paths, DB query meta)
- The MCP server may add presentation metadata (response type, attachments, suggested follow-ups).

10) MCP server → MCP client: transport back
- The server returns HTTP/gRPC reply to the client:
  - Status: 200 OK (or error codes if failures)
  - Body: structured JSON with answer, citations, tool list, and any compact data returned by the REPL (e.g., JSON summary).
- Any post-request hooks run: PostToolUse hooks may track accessed files and call rlm_inject (docs mention a PostToolUse hook that calls rlm_inject to track files).
- If the request triggered file changes or code generation, developer workflow notes: files that modify server behavior should be pushed to both repositories (see Dual-Repo note below).

11) MCP client → Claude Desktop: display
- The snipara-mcp client receives the response and gives it back to Claude Desktop (via the client’s stdout/IPC integration).
- Claude displays the answer to the user, including any inline citations or attachments.
- The local client may also cache or store the session state locally to support follow-ups.

What gets logged & where
- RLM trajectory logs (rlm-runtime): JSONL trajectory files containing plan, calls, time stamps, tool summaries (kept for debugging and auditing).
- MCP server logs: access logs, auth events, request/response metadata, error traces (auth.py, server.py).
- DB logs: queries, slow queries, authentication attempts.
- Usage & billing logs: usage.py increments counters for billing.
- Hooks: PreToolUse/PostToolUse hooks may cause additional metadata to be written (e.g., which docs were read).
- Sensitive data practices: RLM compaction masks secrets and only returns compact results to the model, not raw big datasets.

Network calls, headers, and tokens (example)
- Client → MCP server:
  - POST https://api.snipara.com/v1/mcp/query
  - Headers:
    - Authorization: Bearer <access_token> (or x-api-key: <SNIPARA_API_KEY>)
    - X-Project-Id: <project-id>
    - Content-Type: application/json
  - Body: { "query": "...", "conversation": [...], "session_id": "..." }
- MCP server → rlm-runtime (local or service):
  - Internal IPC or HTTP call with server-side service account token.
- rlm-runtime → snipara tools (context_query):
  - Authenticated API calls to Snipara context APIs (same bearer or internal tokens).
- REPL → DB:
  - Uses DB credentials from config (db.py / config.py), likely over an internal network (TLS).

Special cases & failures
- Invalid/expired tokens: tool calls like rlm_context_query will fail with 401 and ask to re-authenticate (docs example). The client or server should handle token refresh via refresh_token or route user through device flow again.
- Long-running jobs: rlm-runtime manages timeouts and resource limits; Docker REPL will enforce CPU/memory/time limits.
- Sensitive data exposure: RLM and server enforce policies to avoid returning raw data dumps — only compact JSON or aggregates are returned when appropriate.

Development / deployment notes relevant to MCP server changes
- Dual-repo push (MANDATORY):
  - When modifying files in apps/mcp-server/src/ you must push to two repos:
    1. RLMSaas (this repo at github.com/alopez3006/snipara) — push here first (deploys to Vercel + PyPI).

Operational & security practices called out in docs
- REPL sandboxing:
  - Local REPL (RestrictedPython) for trusted execution.

8) Service layer → Database
- Modules: services/*.py, models.py, db.py
- Flow:
  - Business logic in services/*.py constructs a query or ORM call using models.py entities.

- Services transform DB rows to the API response shape.

- Auth failure: auth.py returns 401 → client prompted to re-authenticate (or falls back to OAuth Device Flow).
- Tool failure (rlm tools): rlm_engine returns structured error; may include degraded fallback (e.g., return raw DB rows).

- Token budget exceeded: RLM aborts or degrades to a smaller plan, informs user.

Short answer — available options
- Project API key (X-API-Key: rlm_...)
- Team API key (X-API-Key: rlm_...) — same header format, broader scope
- OAuth access token (Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...) — obtained with the OAuth Device Flow (RFC 8628)
- Local CLI-stored OAuth tokens (~/.snipara/tokens.json) which the client will use automatically (can be removed to force API‑key usage)

Where each is accepted
- MCP server endpoints: accept both OAuth (Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...) and API keys (X-API-Key: rlm_...)
- Inject API endpoints (project automation): use X-API-Key only

Details and security differences

1) Project API Key (X-API-Key: rlm_...)
- Format/handling: keys start with rlm_. Server stores a SHA‑256 hash of the key and looks up the hash (validate_api_key()).
- Scope: project-level (only that project). If project keys are not found, server falls back to team keys.
- Lifetime & revocation: long‑lived until you revoke/rotate in the dashboard.
- Security pros: simple for automation, easy to use in scripts/CI.
- Security cons: long‑lived bearer secret — if leaked, immediate access to project. Needs careful secret storage. Less fine-grained than OAuth scopes (unless server-side enforces additional metadata).
- Best practice: store securely (secrets manager/CI vars), rotate regularly, don’t check into repos. Only first 8 chars shown in UI/logs to limit exposure.

2) Team API Key (X-API-Key: rlm_...)
- Same transport/format as project key but wider scope (access to multiple projects in the team).
- Security tradeoff: more powerful — more risky if leaked. Use only when necessary.

3) OAuth Access Token (Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...)
- Format/handling: tokens start with snipara_at_. Validation uses DB lookup + expiry check (validate_oauth_token()).
- Acquisition: Device Flow (CLI shows a user code and URL), or via refresh tokens. Standard OAuth refresh endpoint exists (/api/oauth/token) and device-token polling endpoint (/api/oauth/device/token).
- Scope & granularity: supports scopes and read/write enforcement; usually more fine-grained per-user and per-token.
- Lifetime & revocation: short(er)-lived access tokens with refresh tokens. Tokens can be individually revoked (DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[tokenId]) and are discoverable via GET /api/settings/tokens.
- Security pros: short-lived, revocable, scoped, tied to a user session — better for interactive user authentication and least-privilege patterns. Device flow avoids copying long-lived secrets.
- Security cons: client must protect refresh tokens; local storage (e.g., ~/.snipara/tokens.json) is a potential attack surface if the machine is compromised.

4) Local CLI-stored OAuth tokens (~/.snipara/tokens.json)
- Behavior: CLI checks for stored OAuth tokens first (priority over API key). Removing or moving that file forces the CLI to use API key mode (or prompt flow).
- Security: convenient but means local file security matters. If you want to force API key usage, you can rm or mv that file.

Other operational notes
- Transport: API key via X-API-Key header; OAuth via Authorization: Bearer header.
- Token refresh: use POST /api/oauth/token with refresh_token to obtain new access_token and refresh_token.
- Error handling: expired/invalid OAuth token returns HTTP 401 with guidance to re-authenticate.
- Revocation: DELETE /api/settings/tokens/[tokenId] revokes OAuth tokens.

Summary guidance (when to use which)
- Interactive users / human sessions: prefer OAuth device flow (short-lived, scoped, revocable).
- Automation / CI / server-to-server: use project API keys (or team keys if necessary), but store them securely and rotate regularly.
- Minimize privileges: use project keys over team keys where possible, and use scoped OAuth tokens if you need fine-grained user-scoped permissions.
- If a token/key is suspected compromised: revoke it immediately (dashboard or DELETE token endpoint), rotate API keys, and invalidate local token files.

  - Header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
  - Format: access tokens start with "snipara_at_" and refresh tokens with "snipara_rt_".
  - Flow: device code flow (RFC 8628) — CLI shows code, user authorizes in browser, client polls /api/oauth/device/token for the access token. Tokens are short(er) lived and refreshable via /api/oauth/token.
  - Scopes: enforced server-side (read/write scopes, per auth.py notes).
  - Lifespan & revocation: access tokens expire; refresh tokens can be used to get new access tokens; tokens can be listed and revoked via GET/DELETE /api/settings/tokens.
  - Security considerations:
    - Shorter lived, scoped, and revocable — generally safer for user-level access.
    - Tied to user session and scopes, so compromise tends to be less damaging than an all-powerful long‑lived key (depending on scope).

  - Stored locally: CLI stores tokens in ~/.snipara/tokens.json (the client will prefer stored OAuth token over API key unless you remove it).
  - Error behavior: invalid/expired OAuth tokens produce a 401 with a message prompting re-authentication.

- MCP vs Inject API differences

  - Choose OAuth for user-level interactions and APIs that accept it; use API keys for automation or Inject API.

  - OAuth tokens are validated by format, DB lookup and expiry checks.

Recommendations / when to use which
  - Use OAuth device flow for interactive, user-scoped access where you want scopes, short-lived tokens, and easy revocation.
  - Use project-scoped API keys for automation tied to a single project (least privilege).
  - Avoid broad team keys for automation unless necessary — they increase blast radius.

- Formats & storage: Keys use an rlm_ prefix. The server stores/verifies only a SHA-256 hash of the key (reduces risk if DB dump occurs).

- Use OAuth Device Flow for interactive/user-level access: gives short-lived tokens, scopes, and central revocation. Better when you want per-user auditing and least-privilege interactive sign-in.
- Use Project API Keys for automation tied to a single project (CI, servers) where you control the key lifecycle; prefer project keys over team keys.

Short answer
- When you query with shared context, the system splits the total token budget between project docs and shared-collection docs, then further splits the shared portion by document category (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE). Documents are included until each category’s allocated token budget is exhausted; remaining docs are marked as not allocated.

How it works (step‑by‑step)
1. Shared context merged first
   - rlm_shared_context merges all linked collections (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE). Shared content is considered before project docs when building the response context.

2. Budget split across buckets
   - The allocation implementation uses a DEFAULT_ALLOCATION that divides the total token budget across:
     - project: 0.40 (40% for project-specific docs)
     - MANDATORY: 0.25 (25%)
     - BEST_PRACTICES: 0.20 (20%)
     - GUIDELINES: 0.10 (10%)
     - REFERENCE: 0.05 (5%)
   - This allocation can be customized (the allocateTokenBudget function accepts a customAllocation override).

   Note: there are user-facing query parameters (e.g., shared_context_budget_percent / include_shared_context) that affect how shared vs project budgets are applied at the rlm_context_query level. The service code then applies the per-category allocation when selecting documents.

3. Allocation within each bucket
   - For project docs: compute projectBudget = floor(totalBudget * allocation.project) and try to include project docs first.
   - For each shared category: compute categoryBudget = floor(totalBudget * allocation[category]) and try to include docs from that category.
   - Within a bucket, docs are sorted by relevanceScore (if present) and then included one-by-one until the bucket’s token budget is filled. Included docs are marked allocated=true with an allocationReason (e.g., "project allocation" or "MANDATORY allocation"). Docs that don’t fit are marked allocated=false with allocationReason "exceeded budget".

4. Hard limits and truncation
   - Collection-level hard limits are enforced (examples from code):
     - MAX_COLLECTIONS_PER_PROJECT: 10
     - MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION: 100,000
     - MAX_DOCS_PER_COLLECTION: 50
     - MIN_RELEVANCE_THRESHOLD: 0.2
   - If a collection exceeds the token cap, enforceCollectionLimits truncates the collection by keeping documents until the collection token cap is reached.

5. Query-level controls
   - rlm_context_query and rlm_shared_context accept parameters such as max_tokens, categories to include, and shared_context_budget_percent (in the MCP Query Order docs). These let you adjust how much budget is reserved for shared context vs project docs.

Practical implications
- MANDATORY and high-priority shared docs are more likely to be included because they get dedicated category budgets.
- Project docs are allocated their own budget slice and are included before some lower-priority shared docs.
- You can override allocations (via query parameters or customAllocation) to change priority if necessary.

- Snipara Cloud (recommended): enable the “real‑time / collaboration” option in the project dashboard (Cloud/Enterprise only). The MCP SSE/WS endpoint is used by clients to join real‑time sessions.
- Self‑hosted: run the MCP server or the universal bridge (rlm-bridge) that proxies to Snipara Cloud, or run your own MCP server implementation that supports SSE/WebSocket and CRDT operation messages.

2) Configure the MCP transport (SSE or WebSocket)
- Use an SSE or WebSocket transport for low‑latency streaming of ops/presence. The docs show an SSE example used by Continue.dev:
  {
    "models": [...],
    "mcpServers": [
      {
        "name": "rlm",
        "transport": {
          "type": "sse",
          "url": "https://api.rlmsaas.com/v1/PROJECT_ID/mcp/sse",
          "headers": {
            "X-API-Key": "{{RLM_API_KEY}}"
          }
        }
      }
    ]
  }
- For WebSocket, the URL and header pattern is similar but uses ws:// or wss:// and an appropriate auth header or query param.

3) Client-side: choose a CRDT/OT library and wire it to MCP
- Use Yjs or Automerge (Yjs is common for low-latency collaboration in editors).
- Each client:
  - Connects to the MCP SSE/WS transport for the project.
  - Translates local CRDT updates into MCP “ops” messages and sends them to the server.
  - Applies ops received from MCP into the local CRDT document.
- Ensure each client includes a stable client ID and user metadata (name, avatar) for presence and conflict reconciliation.

4) Presence and awareness
- Use a small presence/awareness channel so clients can show cursors/selection and a list of connected users.
- Presence can be pushed as lightweight SSE/WS messages (e.g., every N seconds or on cursor change).
- On the server, broadcast presence updates to the session participants.

5) Server-side: persist and broadcast ops
- The MCP server/bridge should:
  - Accept ops from authenticated clients.
  - Validate and optionally transform/sequence ops (if you use OT) or persist CRDT state diffs.
  - Persist the canonical document state (DB, Redis, or blob store).
  - Broadcast ops/state diffs to all subscribed clients.
- For scalability, use sharded session storage and a message broker (Redis pub/sub, NATS, or Kafka) to fan out updates.

6) Authentication and access control
- Use project API keys for machine-to-machine connections and user tokens (JWT/OAuth) for end users.
- Enforce per‑document ACLs on the MCP server (read/write roles).
- Ensure the SSE/WS endpoint requires valid auth headers (e.g., X-API-Key or Authorization: Bearer <token>).

7) Integrations and editors
- Snipara’s VS Code extension exists and can be configured to connect to the MCP server (install snipara-vscode, set API key + project slug). The extension exposes session context and results; for full collaborative editing you wire the extension to the session transport or use a web‑based editor that implements Yjs + MCP.
- If you need browser editors, use TipTap/ProseMirror or CodeMirror + Yjs bindings and connect their update channel to MCP.

8) Operational items / performance
- Heartbeats/timeouts: close inactive sessions to free memory.
- Delta compression: batch small ops or compress diffs.
- Conflict handling: prefer CRDT for simpler conflict-free merges.
- Backups: store periodic snapshots of canonical CRDT state.
- Monitoring: track packet rates, op latency, and active sessions.

Checklist / minimal working setup (quick)
- Project in Snipara with real‑time enabled (Cloud or enterprise).
- SSE/WS MCP endpoint configured with auth (see JSON above).
- Client implementation:
  - Connect to SSE/WS,
  - Use Yjs for document state,
  - Send/receive ops via MCP messages.
- Server/bridge:
  - Receive ops, persist snapshot, broadcast to subscribers,
  - Auth + ACL enforcement,
  - Presence channel.

Troubleshooting tips
- Nothing syncs: confirm clients connect to the correct project endpoint and use the correct API key; check CORS for browser clients.
- Conflicts / lost markers: use CRDT (Yjs) instead of naive OT, ensure ops are correctly ordered or applied.
- High latency: switch from long‑poll to SSE/WS; batch small operations.

Short answer
- The API returns HTTP 429 (Too Many Requests) when you exceed limits. The response includes an error and rate/usage details and rate-limit headers telling you how long to wait.

What the response looks like
- Monthly query limit exceeded (example):
  {
    "success": false,
    "error": "Monthly query limit exceeded",
    "usage": {
      "current": 101,
      "max": 100,
      "exceeded": true,
      "reset_at": "2026-02-01T00:00:00Z"
    }
  }
  - Key fields: usage.current, usage.max (−1 = unlimited), usage.exceeded (true/false), usage.reset_at (ISO timestamp when monthly quota resets).

- Generic 429 / rate limit response examples:
  {
    "error": {
      "code": "RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED",
      "message": "Rate limit exceeded. Try again in 60 seconds"
    }
  }
  or
  {
    "error": {
      "code": "INSUFFICIENT_CREDITS",
      "message": "Need 5000 credits, have 5000"
    }
  }

Headers included
- Every response includes rate-limit headers you should inspect:
  - X-RateLimit-Limit — maximum requests/minute
  - X-RateLimit-Remaining — remaining requests in current window
  - X-RateLimit-Reset — seconds until the minute window resets

Recommended handling
- Check X-RateLimit-Remaining and X-RateLimit-Reset to decide whether to retry.
- Implement exponential backoff on 429 responses.
- Respect the usage.reset_at field for monthly limits (wait until that time or upgrade).
- Reduce requests by caching responses and batching queries where possible.
- If hitting monthly quota frequently, consider upgrading your plan.

Extra notes
- Some endpoints/details may return an INS UFFICIENT_CREDITS style error when credits are lacking.
- For per-minute vs monthly limits the response may include different fields/messages but the action is the same: stop/retry after the indicated reset or upgrade.

- If rlm_context_query finds no relevant documents it returns an empty result set (no sections, total_tokens = 0) and the caller/ UI should show an “empty state” with helpful guidance and suggested next actions.

What the engine does (implementation behavior)
- Searches (keyword/semantic/hybrid) and ranks candidates.

- The response still includes session_context (if any) and a suggestions list produced by the helper (_generate_suggestions). Suggestions typically recommend actions like broadening the query, checking the docs index, or pointing to likely files to inspect.

- Permissions or indexing issues (documents not visible to the querying session).
- Authentication/connection failure to the Snipara API (e.g., OAuth token expired).
